question: A handler checks `if user.role in {"admin","editor"}` and then returns `db.documents.get(doc_id)`. Any editor can now read any document by changing the id. What kind of failure is this?
options: An authentication failure — the user isn't properly logged in

Broken object-level authorization (the IDOR): the code checks that the user may use the endpoint (route-level) but never checks that they may access THIS specific document (object-level). The role check is necessary but not sufficient; you must also verify ownership or a sharing relationship for doc_id. This is the #1 API vulnerability
A rate-limiting failure
Nothing — role-based checks are always sufficient
explanation: Route-level authorization answers 'may this user call this endpoint?' Object-level authorization answers 'may this user act on this particular record?' Passing the first while skipping the second is Broken Object-Level Authorization (IDOR) — OWASP API Security #1. The fix is to authorize the object itself (owner/shared-with) on every access, not just to confirm the user has a role.

question: What does RBAC give you over per-resource ACLs, and where does it break down?
options: RBAC encrypts permissions; ACLs don't

RBAC adds a layer of indirection — users get roles, roles get permissions — so you grant a capability once to a role instead of editing millions of per-resource entries, and onboarding is 'assign a role.' It breaks down when rules depend on the specific object or context ('edit only YOUR documents,' 'refunds only in business hours'): roles are coarse, so teams mint ever-more-specific roles, which is role explosion
RBAC works only for admins; ACLs work for everyone
There is no difference; they're two names for the same thing
explanation: RBAC scales administration by grouping permissions under roles, which is why it's the workhorse for coarse access. Its limitation is granularity: a role can't express object-specific or contextual conditions, so those requirements spawn near-duplicate roles (role explosion) until the model is unmanageable. That's the signal to add ABAC or ReBAC.

question: What distinguishes ReBAC (the Zanzibar model) from ABAC, and when is ReBAC the better fit?
options: ReBAC is just ABAC with a different name

ABAC decides via a boolean over attributes (department, time, sensitivity). ReBAC models permissions as a graph of relationships and answers by finding a path — 'alice is a member of group eng, which is an editor of doc 42, so alice can edit it.' ReBAC fits sharing, folders, groups, and hierarchies naturally (it's how Google Docs/Drive authorize at scale via Zanzibar/OpenFGA/SpiceDB), which ABAC and RBAC express awkwardly
ReBAC only works for two users at a time
ABAC is for databases and ReBAC is for files
explanation: ABAC evaluates attribute predicates; ReBAC traverses relationship tuples. When access is fundamentally about relationships between users and objects — ownership, membership, sharing, folder hierarchies — ReBAC expresses it directly and scales, which is why Zanzibar and its open implementations (OpenFGA, SpiceDB, Ory Keto) power document-sharing products. Real systems often combine RBAC (coarse gates), ABAC (context), and ReBAC (sharing).

question: Why separate the Policy Enforcement Point (PEP) from the Policy Decision Point (PDP), and what must the default decision be?
options: For performance only; there's no security reason

Separating enforcement (the code that intercepts a request and applies the verdict) from the decision (the logic that computes allow/deny from a model) makes authorization centralized, auditable ('who can do what' has one answer), changeable without touching every handler, and complete (one choke point every request passes). The default must be DENY — access requires an explicit allow, never merely the absence of a deny (fail closed)
So that clients can make the decision themselves
The default should be allow, to avoid breaking features
explanation: The PEP/PDP split turns scattered inline checks into one auditable, evolvable decision point — which is exactly what lets you answer questions and change policy safely. Deny-by-default is the fail-closed principle applied per object: an unspecified access is refused, so forgetting to write an allow rule denies rather than exposes. Allow-by-default turns every missing rule into a hole.

question: Why must the authorization decision be computed from server-side state rather than from a role claim in the request — even a validly signed JWT that says `role: admin`?
options: It doesn't matter; a signed JWT is trustworthy, so its role claim can be used directly for access decisions

A signed JWT proves the claim wasn't tampered with, but the authorization decision still needs the CURRENT, authoritative state — a token's role can be stale (the user was demoted after the token was issued, and a stateless token isn't revoked until it expires), and object-level facts like ownership and sharing simply aren't in the token. Decide from server-side data (roles, ownership, relationships) at the time of the request, using the token only to identify the subject
Because JWTs can't contain a role field
Because the client can always forge a signed JWT
explanation: Signature validity is about integrity, not freshness or completeness. A user demoted from admin still holds a validly signed token asserting `role: admin` until it expires; trusting that claim grants access that should be gone. And per-object authorization (does alice own doc 42?) is server-side data the token never carries. Use the token to establish identity, then compute the decision from current server state.

question: Your team keeps minting roles: `editor`, `editor_contractor`, `editor_contractor_non_confidential`, `editor_eu_only`. What's happening, and what's the cleaner approach?
options: This is healthy, granular RBAC; keep adding roles as needed

This is role explosion — encoding object- and context-specific conditions into role names because RBAC can't express them. The cleaner approach is to move those conditions into a model built for them: ABAC for context/attributes ('allow if not resource.confidential and subject.region == "EU"') and/or ReBAC for object relationships, keeping RBAC only for coarse gates. The rules become explicit policy instead of a combinatorial explosion of roles
Switch everything to per-resource ACLs
Delete all roles and allow every logged-in user
explanation: Role names that encode attributes and exceptions are role explosion — a symptom of forcing fine-grained, contextual rules into a coarse model. Expressing 'non-confidential', 'EU only', or 'contractor' as attribute conditions (ABAC) or relationships (ReBAC) makes the policy readable, auditable, and non-combinatorial. Most mature systems layer the models: RBAC for broad gates, ABAC for context, ReBAC for sharing and hierarchy.
